name: Waterman (Corners First)
description: A corners-first method developed by Marc Waterman in 1981. Unlike most modern methods that build layers or blocks, Waterman solves all corners first, then uses M-slice and U moves to solve the remaining edges. It was one of the fastest methods of its era and remains a fascinating alternative approach to the cube. The method rewards strong understanding of corner permutation and edge manipulation.

steps.name: First Layer Corners
steps.description: Solve all four corners of the first layer (typically the bottom layer). This can be done intuitively or with short algorithms.
tips: Use color neutrality to find easy cases

steps.name: Last Layer Corners
steps.description: Solve all four corners of the last layer while preserving the first layer corners. This involves orienting and permuting the U-layer corners.
tips: CLL algorithms solve orientation and permutation simultaneously
Learn to recognize corner cases quickly

steps.name: Edge Orientation
steps.description: Orient all twelve edges so they can be solved without F or B moves. This step ensures the remaining solve uses only M, U, and slice moves.
tips: This step is similar to EO in ZZ and Petrus
Usually only a few edges need flipping

steps.name: Last Layer Edges
steps.description: Place the four edges of the last layer into their correct positions using M and U moves.
tips: ELL algorithms handle all edge-only last layer cases
Recognition is based on edge positions relative to solved corners

steps.name: Middle and First Layer Edges
steps.description: Solve the remaining eight edges (four middle layer, four first layer) using M and U moves. Since edges are oriented, this step is efficient and ergonomic.
tips: Use M and U generators to cycle edges into place
Plan ahead to avoid disrupting solved edges
This step can be broken into sub-steps for easier learning

pros: Unique solving perspective teaches deep cube understanding
Ergonomic M and U slice moves in later steps
cons: Very few modern resources or tutorials
Corner-first approach feels unintuitive to most solvers
Difficult to plan ahead due to non-standard order
Not competitive with modern methods for speedsolving
creator: Marc Waterman
